ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper proposes the first probabilistic multi-prototype embedding (representing words as Gaussian mixtures, w2gm), which clearly distinguishes it from prior unimodal Gaussian embeddings (w2g) and earlier non-probabilistic multiprototype models (Huang et al., Tian et al., Chen et al.). Using the expected likelihood kernel as a tractable energy for mixtures and training with a max-margin ranking objective is a non-trivial technical extension to existing methods. The contribution is a notable and clearly differentiated novelty that would inform experts, though it builds on well-established ideas in embeddings and mixture models rather than introducing an entirely new paradigm. | B(4): The paper proposes a genuinely new combination: the first probabilistic multi-prototype embedding (w2gm) that represents words as Gaussian mixtures, extending unimodal Gaussian embeddings (w2g) and prior multiprototype vector models. It introduces a tractable energy (expected likelihood kernel) and a max-margin training objective tailored to mixtures, addressing the documented limitation of large variances in unimodal Gaussians and the intractability of using KL for mixtures. While the approach is a clear, non-trivial extension of existing Gaussian and multiprototype work and is well differentiated from prior baselines (w2g, Huang et al., Tian et al.), it builds on established ideas enough that its novelty is notable but not revolutionary.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper proposes the first probabilistic multi-prototype embedding (representing words as Gaussian mixtures, w2gm), which clearly distinguishes it from prior unimodal Gaussian embeddings (w2g) and earlier non-probabilistic multiprototype models (Huang et al., Tian et al., Chen et al.). Using the expected likelihood kernel as a tractable energy for mixtures and training with a max-margin ranking objective is a non-trivial technical extension to existing methods. The contribution is a notable and clearly differentiated novelty that would inform experts, though it builds on well-established ideas in embeddings and mixture models rather than introducing an entirely new paradigm.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper proposes a genuinely new combination: the first probabilistic multi-prototype embedding (w2gm) that represents words as Gaussian mixtures, extending unimodal Gaussian embeddings (w2g) and prior multiprototype vector models. It introduces a tractable energy (expected likelihood kernel) and a max-margin training objective tailored to mixtures, addressing the documented limitation of large variances in unimodal Gaussians and the intractability of using KL for mixtures. While the approach is a clear, non-trivial extension of existing Gaussian and multiprototype work and is well differentiated from prior baselines (w2g, Huang et al., Tian et al.), it builds on established ideas enough that its novelty is notable but not revolutionary.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=5] A(5): This paper is well within the scope of top NLP/ML venues: it proposes a probabilistic multi-prototype word embedding (Gaussian mixtures) addressing polysemy, uses a principled analytic energy (expected likelihood kernel) and a max-margin ranking objective, and directly targets the word-representation community. The evaluation follows community standards (SimLex, WS, SCWS, word entailment datasets) and compares to appropriate baselines (word2vec skip-gram, w2g, and multiprototype prior work). The formulation, methods, and evaluation metrics (Spearman, AP, F1) match expectations for a top-tier conference, and the paper is positioned correctly within the NLP/representation learning literature. | B(5): This paper addresses probabilistic multi-prototype word embeddings (Gaussian mixtures) — a core topic in NLP/ML representation learning — and therefore fits squarely within top NLP/ML venues. Its formulation (w2gm with an expected likelihood kernel trained by a max-margin ranking objective) targets the right audience and standards for such conferences. Evaluation uses standard datasets (SimLex, WS, SCWS, Word entailment, etc.) and metrics (Spearman, AP, F1) and compares against appropriate baselines (word2vec, w2g, prior multiprototype methods), matching expected methodological and evaluation practices. Related work coverage is thorough and the paper is positioned correctly within the community.

The evaluation follows community standards (SimLex, WS, SCWS, word entailment datasets) and compares to appropriate baselines (word2vec skip-gram, w2g, and multiprototype prior work). The formulation, methods, and evaluation metrics (Spearman, AP, F1) match expectations for a top-tier conference, and the paper is positioned correctly within the NLP/representation learning literature.

SUBSTANCE.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The experimental evaluation is broad and compares the proposed w2gm against strong baselines (word2vec skip-gram, w2g, and several multiprototype methods) across many standard benchmarks (SimLex, WS variants, MEN, SCWS, Baroni entailment data, and a 1B-token Wikipedia corpus), supporting the main claims. However, no ablation studies or sensitivity analyses are reported and hyperparameters/code are not mentioned, which weakens reproducibility and understanding of which components drive gains. The paper does acknowledge some limitations (e.g., KL divergence lacks a closed form for mixtures and unimodal Gaussians inflate variance for polysemy) but does not appear to analyze broader failure cases or negative results in depth. | B(3): The paper evaluates w2gm on a wide range of word-similarity and entailment datasets (SimLex, WordSim, MEN, SCWS, Baroni et al. entailment, etc.) and compares against strong baselines (word2vec, w2g, multiple multiprototype methods), so experimental coverage of tasks and baselines is broad. However, ablation studies or sensitivity analyses are not reported in the summary, and only a couple of limitations are noted (KL intractability for mixtures, unimodal variance issue), suggesting limited failure-mode analysis. Reproducibility is weak because hyperparameters and code availability are not reported. Overall the experiments are adequate but missing important analyses and reproducibility details.

IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): This paper tackles the important problem of polysemy by introducing the first probabilistic multi-prototype embeddings (w2gm) represented as Gaussian mixtures and an analytic expected likelihood kernel trained with a max-margin objective. It demonstrably reduces per-meaning variance relative to unimodal Gaussian embeddings and models asymmetric entailment relationships better, with empirical improvements over strong baselines (word2vec, w2g) on multiple similarity and entailment benchmarks using large corpora. The approach is likely to influence future work on probabilistic/uncertainty-aware embeddings and asymmetric relations and will be widely built upon, though it extends rather than entirely replaces existing multiprototype ideas. | B(4): This paper targets an important problem—modeling polysemy—by introducing the first probabilistic multi-prototype embedding (w2gm) that represents words as Gaussian mixtures and uses a tractable expected likelihood kernel for training. It demonstrates empirical gains over strong baselines (word2vec skip-gram and unimodal Gaussian w2g) across multiple standard benchmarks (SimLex, WS, MEN, SCWS, and a word entailment dataset), so the approach is likely to influence how researchers model multiple senses and asymmetric relations like entailment. The method opens substantial new research directions in probabilistic multi-sense representations and downstream semantic tasks, though practical issues (e.g., no closed-form KL for mixtures) may temper immediate, wide adoption.

reviewer_a.justification: This paper tackles the important problem of polysemy by introducing the first probabilistic multi-prototype embeddings (w2gm) represented as Gaussian mixtures and an analytic expected likelihood kernel trained with a max-margin objective. It demonstrably reduces per-meaning variance relative to unimodal Gaussian embeddings and models asymmetric entailment relationships better, with empirical improvements over strong baselines (word2vec, w2g) on multiple similarity and entailment benchmarks using large corpora. The approach is likely to influence future work on probabilistic/uncertainty-aware embeddings and asymmetric relations and will be widely built upon, though it extends rather than entirely replaces existing multiprototype ideas.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper targets an important problem—modeling polysemy—by introducing the first probabilistic multi-prototype embedding (w2gm) that represents words as Gaussian mixtures and uses a tractable expected likelihood kernel for training. It demonstrates empirical gains over strong baselines (word2vec skip-gram and unimodal Gaussian w2g) across multiple standard benchmarks (SimLex, WS, MEN, SCWS, and a word entailment dataset), so the approach is likely to influence how researchers model multiple senses and asymmetric relations like entailment. The method opens substantial new research directions in probabilistic multi-sense representations and downstream semantic tasks, though practical issues (e.g., no closed-form KL for mixtures) may temper immediate, wide adoption.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper’s main claims are clearly stated and appear supported by broad experimental evidence across many standard similarity and entailment datasets and sensible baselines (word2vec, w2g, prior multiprototype methods). The mathematical choice of the expected likelihood kernel as a closed-form energy for Gaussian mixtures and the note that KL has no closed form for mixtures are technically correct. However, the experimental methodology omits reporting hyperparameters (and no statistical significance testing is mentioned), which is a notable omission that reduces reproducibility and confidence though it does not obviously invalidate the main technical claims. | B(4): The paper's main claims are clearly stated and largely supported: representing words as Gaussian mixtures (w2gm) and using the expected likelihood kernel is mathematically sensible (the ELK for mixtures reduces to closed‑form pairwise Gaussian inner products) and the authors evaluate on a wide suite of standard benchmarks against appropriate baselines (word2vec, w2g, and multiprototype methods). They correctly note the KL divergence lacks a closed form for mixtures and avoid it, and report empirical improvements including reduced per‑meaning variance and better entailment modeling. However, important experimental details are missing in the summary (no hyperparameter reporting, no mention of statistical significance tests or code release), which are minor but leave some uncertainty about reproducibility and the robustness of claimed gains.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper’s main claims are clearly stated and appear supported by broad experimental evidence across many standard similarity and entailment datasets and sensible baselines (word2vec, w2g, prior multiprototype methods). The mathematical choice of the expected likelihood kernel as a closed-form energy for Gaussian mixtures and the note that KL has no closed form for mixtures are technically correct. However, the experimental methodology omits reporting hyperparameters (and no statistical significance testing is mentioned), which is a notable omission that reduces reproducibility and confidence though it does not obviously invalidate the main technical claims.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper's main claims are clearly stated and largely supported: representing words as Gaussian mixtures (w2gm) and using the expected likelihood kernel is mathematically sensible (the ELK for mixtures reduces to closed‑form pairwise Gaussian inner products) and the authors evaluate on a wide suite of standard benchmarks against appropriate baselines (word2vec, w2g, and multiprototype methods). They correctly note the KL divergence lacks a closed form for mixtures and avoid it, and report empirical improvements including reduced per‑meaning variance and better entailment modeling. However, important experimental details are missing in the summary (no hyperparameter reporting, no mention of statistical significance tests or code release), which are minor but leave some uncertainty about reproducibility and the robustness of claimed gains.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper compares against appropriate and relevant baselines (word2vec skip-gram, w2g/Vilnis & McCallum 2014, and multiple multiprototype methods such as Huang 2012, Tian et al. 2012/2014, Chen et al. 2014) and evaluates on many standard datasets (SimLex, WS variants, MEN, SCWS, Baroni entailment, etc.) using standard metrics (Spearman, AP, F1). Differences between the proposed w2gm and baselines are analyzed (per-meaning variance reduction, asymmetric entailment modeling), and related work coverage is described as thorough. Minor concerns that prevent a top score: hyperparameters are not reported (which may affect fairness of comparisons), and the summary does not indicate whether the paper explicitly acknowledges cases where prior methods perform comparably or better. | B(4): The paper compares against appropriate and recent baselines, including word2vec skip-gram, the Vilnis & McCallum Gaussian embeddings (w2g), and multiple prior multiprototype methods (Huang et al., Tian et al., Chen et al.), and evaluates on a wide set of standard benchmarks (SimLex, WS variants, SCWS, word entailment, etc.) using standard metrics (Spearman, AP, F1). These choices indicate broadly fair and relevant comparisons under common evaluation conditions. However, the summary notes hyperparameters/code are not reported and does not explicitly state whether the authors acknowledge cases where prior methods perform comparably or better, which are minor omissions affecting transparency of some comparative claims.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper compares against appropriate and relevant baselines (word2vec skip-gram, w2g/Vilnis & McCallum 2014, and multiple multiprototype methods such as Huang 2012, Tian et al. 2012/2014, Chen et al. 2014) and evaluates on many standard datasets (SimLex, WS variants, MEN, SCWS, Baroni entailment, etc.) using standard metrics (Spearman, AP, F1). Differences between the proposed w2gm and baselines are analyzed (per-meaning variance reduction, asymmetric entailment modeling), and related work coverage is described as thorough. These choices indicate broadly fair and relevant comparisons under common evaluation conditions. However, the summary notes hyperparameters/code are not reported and does not explicitly state whether the authors acknowledge cases where prior methods perform comparably or better, which are minor omissions affecting transparency of some comparative claims.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper appears well-organized with a clear logical flow from method (Gaussian mixtures and the expected likelihood kernel) to experiments and conclusion, and the main contributions are stated early (introducing probabilistic multi-prototype embeddings and the analytic energy). Notation and analytic choices are presented clearly enough to follow (e.g., why KL is not used for mixtures), and related work coverage is described as thorough. Minor presentation issues remain: hyperparameters are not reported and code availability is not mentioned, and the summary does not indicate whether figures/tables are fully effective, which slightly reduces reproducibility and presentation completeness. | B(4): The paper appears well-organized with a logical flow—intro, method (w2gm: Gaussian mixtures, expected likelihood kernel, max-margin objective), experiments on standard benchmarks, and conclusion—and the main contributions are stated early (first probabilistic multi-prototype Gaussian-mixture embedding, analytic energy, training objective). Writing quality is reported as good and related work coverage as thorough, suggesting notation and definitions are generally clear. Minor presentation issues remain: hyperparameters are not reported and code availability is not mentioned, and the summary does not comment on figures/tables, which slightly reduces completeness and reproducibility.

main_claims: Introduce the first probabilistic multi-prototype word embedding that represents each word as a Gaussian mixture (w2gm) to capture multiple meanings (polysemy).
Use the expected likelihood kernel (inner product between distributions) as an analytic, tractable energy function and train the model with a max-margin energy-based ranking objective.
w2gm reduces per-meaning variance for polysemous words compared to unimodal Gaussian embeddings and models entailment better (asymmetric relationships).
w2gm outperforms baselines such as word2vec skip-gram and Gaussian embeddings (w2g) on several benchmark tasks including word similarity and word entailment datasets.
_summary.methodology: Represent each word as a Gaussian mixture (means, covariances, mixture weights) and train parameters with a max-margin ranking objective that maximizes similarity of words and their contexts, using the expected likelihood kernel (closed-form for Gaussian mixtures) as the energy/similarity function.
datasets: MTurk (Radinsky et al. / Halawi et al.)
SCWS (Stanford Contextual Word Similarities, Huang et al., 2012)
baselines: Word to Gaussian (w2g) — Vilnis and McCallum (2014)
limitations: KL divergence does not have a closed form for Gaussian mixtures (so it is not used as the main energy for mixtures).
Unimodal Gaussian embeddings suffer from overly large variances for polysemous words (limitation of prior w2g baseline).
_summary.topic_area: NLP - word embeddings (probabilistic Gaussian mixture embeddings)
